name: Ahmes B
notes: Wife of Thutmose I; known from a range of monuments, principally those of her daughter, Hatshepsut, but also from other material, including a statue of her mortuary priest, Nakht, from Karnak.

name: Ahmose B
notes: Probably a son of Amenhotep II, in office during the reign of Thutmose IV. Known from a stela in Berlin (probably from Heliopolis) and a broken statue in Cairo (probably from Koptos).

name: Akheper[ka?]re
notes: Son of Amenhotep II. Named in tomb TT226.

name: Akheperure
notes: Son of Amenhotep II. Named in graffiti at Konosso and almost certainly in tomb TT226.

name: Amenemhat B
notes: Son and heir of Thutmose III. Named on the south side of Festival Hall at Karnak temple in year 24.

name: Amenemhat C
notes: Son of Thutmose IV. Depicted in tomb TT64, died young and buried with his father in KV43, where his canopic jars and probable mummy were found.

name: Amenemopet A
notes: Probably a son of Amenhotep II; owner of stela C from near Amenhotep II's Sphinx Temple. A stela from Saqqara showing a prince of this name with his nurse, Senetruiu, may refer to him.

name: Amenemopet B
notes: Possible daughter of Thutmose IV. Her mummy was reburied during the 21st Dynasty; its label was once in Bonn.

name: Amenhotep B
notes: Son of Thutmose III, later king as AMENHOTEP II. Depicted in the tomb of Min, Mayor of Thinis (TT109), and perhaps in the anonymous tomb TT143. On a stela from the sphinx at Giza, he states that he came to the throne at the age of 18.

name: Amenhotep C
notes: Son, and probably heir, of Amenhotep II. Frequently mentioned in an administrative papyrus in the British Museum, and probably owner of a stela found near the Sphinx at Giza, from which the owner's name, written in a cartouche, had been erased.

name: Amenhotep D
notes: Son of Thutmose IV, later king as AMENHOTEP III. Appears in the tomb of his tutor Heqaerneheh (TT64), and on two Konosso graffiti.

name: Amenmose
notes: Son and heir of Thutmose I, whose name was written in a cartouche. Owner of a miniature stone shrine, a fragment of which was bought at Giza, dated to year 4 of his father (Louvre). He accompanies his brother, Wadjmose, in a scene in the tomb of Paheri at El-Kab.

name: Beket(amun)
notes: Daughter of Thutmose III; named on a fragment of a faience votive object from Deir el-Bahari (Boston) on a wooden staff (of her servant, Amenmose now in Brooklyn), and probably on a scarab in the British Museum. She may have been represented in the Hathor Chapel of her father at Deir el-Bahari, where the figure of a princess behind Meryetamun C has lost its name.

name: Hatshepsut D
notes: Daughter of Thutmose I, wife of Thutmose II and later king. A range of monuments date to her period as queen, and also as regent for Thutmose III. These include inscriptions from Karnak, Nubia and Sinai, and an (unused) tomb and sarcophagus in the Wadi Siqqat Taqa el-Zeide at Thebes.

name: Henutiunu
notes: Her mummy was reburied during the 21st Dynasty on Sheikh Abd el-Qurna, but robbed prior to 1857; its labels are in Edinburgh.

name: Huy
notes: Mother of Meryetre-Hatshepsut. Represented on a statue in the British Museum, and played an important role in the cults of Amun, Re and Atum.

name: Iaret
notes: Daughter of Amenhotep II and wife of Thutmose IV from around year 7 onwards, which is the date of inscriptions at Serabit el-Khadim and Konosso. The transcription of her name is not certain, it being written with a simple cobra, which has a number of possible readings.

name: Ipu B
notes: Mother of Sitiah. Named on an offering table of her daughter, from Abydos and now in Cairo.

name: Iset A
notes: Mother of Thutmose III; she was given the title of KGW during his reign, as well as GW after her death. Possessor of a statue from Karnak, and mentioned a number of times on her son's funerary monuments and equipment.

name: Iset B
notes: Daughter of Thutmose III and Meryetre-Hatshepsut. Represented on the statue of her grandmother, Huy, in the British Museum.

name: Khaemwaset A
notes: Probable son of Amenhotep II. Named in two Sehel graffiti including the prenomen of Amenhotep II.

name: Menhet
notes: Wife of Thutmose III, probably of Syrian extraction. Buried in a tomb in Wadi Gabbanet el-Qurud together with Menwi and Merti; much of the funerary equipment is now in the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

name: Menkheperre A
notes: Son of Thutmose III and Meryetre-Hatshepsut. He is depicted upon a British Museum statuette of his grandmother, Huy. Canopic jar fragments from the Valley of the Queens, in Strasbourg and Cairo, are probably his.

name: Menwi
notes: Wife of Thutmose III, probably of Syrian extraction. Buried with Menhet and Merti.

name: Merti
notes: Wife of Thutmose III, probably of Syrian extraction. Buried with Menhet and Menwi.

name: Merybennu
notes: Probably a son of Thutmose III or Amenhotep II. Known from a shabti figure in the Oriental Museum, Durham.

name: Meryetamun C
notes: Daughter of Thutmose III and Meryetre-Hatshepsut. Represented on the statuette of her grandmother, Huy, and on the wall of the Hathor shrine of Thutmose III at Deir el-Bahari. She (or Meryetamun D) was also shown on the lap of the Overseer of Works, Benermerut, from Karnak.

name: Meryetamun D
notes: Daughter of Thutmose III and Meryetre-Hatshepsut. Represented on the statue of her grandmother, Huy.

name: Meryetptah A
notes: One of the group of princesses reburied during the 21st Dynasty on Sheikh Abd el-Qurna.

name: Meryetre(-Hatshepsut)
notes: Wife of Thutmose III; daughter of the Adoratrix Huy and mother of Amenhotep II. Known from a number of monuments, including the Edifice of Amenhotep II at Karnak. Many of her representations were usurped by Tiaa A during the reign of Thutmose IV, into which she seems to have survived. Her disgrace under her grandson is also suggested by her apparent non-use of tomb KV42 in the Valley of the Kings, which had previously been taken over for her burial.

name: Mutemwia
notes: Wife of Thutmose IV and mother of Amenhotep III; shown in the 'divine birth' scenes of her son in Luxor temple. A statue of her probably came from his mortuary temple, with a figure of her in a boat found adjacent to the granite sanctuary of the Karnak temple (British Museum); she is also represented with her son on the Colossi of Memnon and in the tomb of Heqareshu (TT226, now in the Luxor Museum).

name: Mutneferet A
notes: Wife of Thutmose I, mother of Thutmose II and probable daughter of Ahmose I. Represented by the leg of her son on a colossus in front of the south face of the Eighth Pylon, in the temple of Thutmose III at Deir el-Bahari and on a stela found near the Ramesseum. She was also the owner of a statue found in the chapel of Wadjmose.

name: Nebetia
notes: Daughter of Prince Siatum A, and probably a granddaughter of Thutmose IV. Her mummy was reburied during the 21st Dynasty on Sheikh Abd el-Qurna with a number of other princesses of the period, but was robbed prior to 1857; its label is in Edinburgh.

name: Nebetiunet B
notes: Daughter of Thutmose III and Meryetre. Represented on the lap of her grandmother, Huy, on the latter's statuette.

name: Nebetnehat A
notes: Wife of a king of the mid-18th Dynasty. Her name is enclosed in a cartouche on canopic fragments from the Valley of the Queens.
spouse_names: a king of the mid-18th Dynasty

name: Nebtu
notes: Wife of Thutmose III. Represented in her husband's tomb, and had an estate whose steward, Nebamun, was buried in tomb TT24.

name: Nedjem
notes: Son of Amenhotep II. Named, with Webensenu, on the statue of Overseer of Works, Minmose, from Karnak.

name: Nefertiry B
notes: Daughter of Thutmose III; depicted on a pillar in the king's tomb (KV34).

name: Nefertiry C
notes: Wife of Thutmose IV. Shown on eight stelae from Giza of her and her husband before deities, a stela from Luxor temple, and named on a scarab from Gurob.

name: Neferubity
notes: Daughter of Thutmose I and Ahmes B. Depicted on the walls of the sanctuary of Hatshepsut's temple at Deir el-Bahari.

name: Neferure A
notes: Daughter of Hatshepsut, and possibly wife of Thutmose III. Depicted on a number of statues of her tutor, Senenmut, on the walls of the Deir el-Bahari temple, and on stelae from Sinai and Karnak. A tomb that may be hers is in the Wadi Qubbet el-Qurud at Thebes.

name: Pyihia
notes: Daughter of Thutmose IV. One of the group of princesses reburied during the 21st Dynasty on Sheikh Abd el-Qurna.

name: Siamun B
notes: Son of Thutmose III. Named upon the statuette of the Chancellor, Sennefer, in the Cairo Museum.

name: Siatum A
notes: Probable son of Thutmose IV. Known from two sources: a relief of his tutor, Meryre, from Saqqara, and a label which names him as the father of a lady (Nebetia) reburied during the 21st Dynasty.

name: Sithori
notes: One of the group of princesses reburied during the 21st Dynasty on Sheikh Abd el-Qurna.

name: Sitiah
notes: Wife of Thutmose III, and perhaps the mother of Amenemhat B; daughter of the nurse Ipu B. A number of items were dedicated to her by her husband after her death.

name: Tatau
notes: One of the group of princesses reburied during the 21st Dynasty on Sheikh Abd el-Qurna.

name: Thutmose A
notes: Son of Amenhotep II, later king as THUTMOSE IV, and conceivably identical with Thutmose Q. Owner of a statue from the Mut temple at Karnak, which also bears the name of his tutor, Heqareshu.

father_name: a king of the mid-18th Dynasty
name: Ti
notes: Daughter of a king of the mid-18th Dynasty. Owner of canopic fragments from the Valley of the Queens.

name: Tiaa A
notes: Wife of Amenhotep II and mother of Thutmose IV. A number of monuments were created for her by the latter at Giza, Thebes and the Fayoum, including: number of usurpations of material belonging to Meryetre-Hatshepsut. She was buried in tomb KV32, where many fragments of her funerary equipment have been found; some material was washed by floodwater into the adjacent tomb KV47, where it was for a long time thought to belong to a like-named mother of Siptah.

name: Tiaa B
notes: Daughter of Thutmose IV. She is probably the owner of three canopic jars from the Valley of the Queens, and also the princess of the name depicted in the tomb of Sobkhotep at Thebes (TT63). Probably one of the group of princesses reburied during the 21st Dynasty on Sheikh Abd el-Qurna.

name: Tintamun
notes: Daughter of Thutmose IV; a fragment of one of her canopic jars was found in tomb KV43 and is now in the Cairo Museum.

name: Wadjmose
notes: Son of Thutmose I, perhaps born only a few years before the king's accession to the throne. His major contemporary attestation is in the tomb of Paheri. Perhaps under his brother, Thutmose II, a chapel was dedicated to him, in between the sites of the later Ramesseum and the mortuary temple of Thutmose IV.

name: Webensenu
notes: Son of Amenhotep II. Died as a child and buried with his father in tomb KV35, where his probable body still lies. Named on the block statue of Minmose, from Karnak.

name: Wiay A
notes: One of the group of princesses reburied during the 21st Dynasty on Sheikh Abd el-Qurna.

name: Amenemhat Q
notes: Unplaced, probably in the earlier part of the dynasty. Owner of a rewrapped mummy found in the cliffs north of tomb TT280 and, probably, of the adjacent tomb MMA 1021.

name: Henut Q
notes: Wife of a king of the mid-18th Dynasty. Her name is enclosed in a cartouche on canopic fragments from the Valley of the Queens.
spouse_names: a king of the mid-18th Dynasty

name: Thutmose Q
notes: Owner of a whip found in the tomb of Tutankhamun; possibly identical with Thutmose A or B, although the latter's titles are otherwise all sacerdotal.

name: [...]pentepkau
notes: Unplaced, probably mid-18th Dynasty. Known only from a fragment of sphinx-stela from near the Second Pyramid of Giza.
